name: Ghibli Museum
description: A magical place that lets you play in and explore the world of Studio Ghibli.

name: Sagano Bamboo Forest
description: The sound of swaying stalks in this stunning grove has been named a governmentally recognized sound.

name: Wisteria Tunnel
description: An exquisite tunnel of cascading flowers.

name: Cat Island
description: On Japan's Tashirojima Island, cats are king.

name: Vampire Café
description: A taste of the macabre Tokyo's ritzy shopping district.

name: Jigokudani Monkey Park
description: Hot springs monkey spa in the middle of Hell's Valley.

name: The Giant Ghibli Clock
description: Tokyo’s only Vaudeville-cuckoo clock–steampunk-Victorian curio cabinet time piece.

name: Omoide Yokocho
description: Colloquially known as “piss alley,” this narrow street filled with tiny bars and barbecue stands looks like it was pulled straight out of “Blade Runner.”

name: Nishiki Market
description: This 700-year-old street food market features baby octopus and soy donuts.

name: Gotokuji Temple
description: The legendary birthplace of the Japanese maneki-neko is dotted with hundreds of lucky beckoning cats.

name: Sanrio Puroland
description: If Hello Kitty were Mickey Mouse, this would be Disneyland.

name: Fushimi Inari-taisha Shrine
description: The 10,000 Torii shrine.

name: Shiratani Unsuikyo Ravine
description: The magical, mossy forest that inspired 'Princess Mononoke.'

name: Godzilla Head
description: Not quite the menace it once was, this monster still has some chops.

name: Aokigahara Forest
description: A beautiful, dense forest in the shadow of Mount Fuji, with a tragic reputation.

name: Yokai Street
description: This neighborhood thoroughfare is haunted by dozens of homemade monsters.

name: Sengaku-Ji
description: This Buddhist temple is home to the graves of the Japanese national legends, the 47 Ronin.

name: Akihabara Electric Town
description: Once a black market electronics marketplace, now an otaku shopper's paradise.

name: Gunkanjima Island
description: Once the most densely populated place in the world, this island is now a ghost town.

name: Otagi Nenbutsu-ji Temple
description: Twelve hundred stone carvings guard this off-the-beaten-path Buddhist temple.

name: Yoro Park
description: Experience park where visitors are told to expect the unexpected.

name: Giant Unicorn GUNDAM Robot
description: A 20-meter (65-foot) robot towers over Tokyo Bay.

name: The Firefly Squid of Toyama Bay
description: Bioluminescent firefly squid light up this Japanese fishing port.

name: Pigment
description: Every color in the rainbow awaits.

name: Tōdai-ji Daibutsuden (The Great Buddha Hall)
description: This massive wooden building contains a giant Buddha, healing pillars, and tame deer wandering the grounds.

name: Nishiyama Onsen Keiunkan
description: The oldest hotel in the world has been operated by the same family for over 1,300 years, even catering to samurai.

name: Kochia Hill
description: Every autumn, this hillside is ablaze with red summer cypress.

name: Alice on Wednesday
description: Go through the looking glass in Tokyo's Wonderland-themed shop.

name: The Vine Bridges of Iya Valley
description: Hanging over one of Japan's "hidden valleys," these vine bridges must be remade every three years.

name: Sanjūsangen-dō
description: 1,001 carved statues of the Buddhist God of Mercy, each with a unique face.

name: 8bit Cafe
description: Transport yourself to the 1980s era of video games at this themed restaurant and bar.

name: Shin-Yokohama Ramen Museum
description: This historical theme park celebrates all things noodle.

name: Sōgenji Temple (Kappa-Dera)
description: Shrine to Japanese water-goblins, complete with their preserved body parts.

name: Yunessun Spa Resort
description: This spa theme park lets its guests take a dip in pools of coffee, wine, or ramen broth.

name: Harukor
description: Tokyo’s only Ainu restaurant serves Indigenous food from northern Japan.

name: Ryōan-ji
description: The quintessential Japanese rock garden has existed for more than 500 years.

name: Narusawa Hyoketsu (Ice Cave)
description: A 70-foot-deep ice cave formed by lava from Mt. Fuji on the edge of Japan's "Suicide Forest."

name: Kojo
description: This subterranean spot might be the epitome of Japan's baroque, retro coffee houses.

name: Blue Pond
description: This mesmerizingly colorful pond looks like a scene straight out of a fairytale.

name: Tenryu-Ji Shrine's Bamboo Trail
description: A beautiful bamboo trail tucked in this famous Japanese shrine.

name: Ninja Akasaka
description: A restaurant modeled after feudal Japan, where the waitstaff are trained as ninjas.

name: Hachiko
description: After a profound show of devotion for his master, a dog becomes the symbol of loyalty for an entire nation.

name: Itsukushima Shrine
description: This Japanese national treasure was originally built in 593.

name: Kyoto Botanical Garden
description: With over 12,000 different species of plants, this botanical garden is Japan's oldest and most diverse.

name: Ekibenya Matsuri
description: A railway station takeout shop sells boxed meals in containers shaped like trains and snowmen that hold the country’s abundant regional delicacies.

name: Kokoen Garden
description: Nine Gardens in one—with tea service.

name: Okunoshima
description: Japanese island famous for poison gas and fluffy bunnies.

name: Jimbōchō Book Town
description: A bibliophile's heaven that contains hundreds of bookstores, offering over a third of secondhand books for sale in Tokyo.

name: Hakone Open-Air Museum
description: Open-air museum with an elaborate sculpture garden, stretching for over 70,000 square-meters.

name: Mohri Garden Pond
description: An ordinary pond with fish from out of this world.

name: Sagano Scenic Railway
description: A train ride to the middle of nowhere gives its passengers some of the most iconic views of Japan's seasons.

name: Monet's Pond
description: This pond is so stunning it looks like you've stepped into one of Monet's masterpieces.

name: Ryūsendō Cave
description: A massive cave complex boasts some of the clearest water (and most charming bats) in the world.

name: Detective Bar Progress
description: The bartenders at this crime-fighting theme cafe are actual private detectives by day.

name: Asakusa Underground Street
description: Stuck in a bygone era, the oldest subterranean shopping street in Japan hides beneath the popular Asakusa district of Tokyo.

name: Starbucks Ninenzaka Yasaka Chaya
description: This Kyoto Starbucks takes coffee drinkers back in time.

name: Shirakawa-go
description: This isolated Japanese mountain village untouched by time showcases unique and stunning traditional architecture.

name: Sokushinbutsu of Dainichibou Temple
description: The self-mummified monks of Japan.

name: Yanaka Cemetery
description: This sprawling scenic burial ground is the final resting place of Japan's last shogun.

name: Chinoike Jigoku
description: Steaming blood-red hot spring from hell.

name: Meguro Parasitological Museum
description: Meet the world's longest tapeworm in a collection that really gets under your skin.

name: Samurai Museum
description: Dedicated to the armor and weapons used by the legendary Japanese warriors.

name: Hachikō's Grave
description: People from around the world visit the shrine of Japan’s legendarily loyal dog.

name: Vowz
description: Drinkslinger is an unlikely vocation for a Buddhist monk, but at this Tokyo bar the enlightenment is served in a martini glass.

name: Tenshou-Kyousha Shrine Mermaid Mummy
description: Reportedly 1,400 years old, this may be the first of the Fiji Mermaids.

name: Gorilla Building
description: A King Kong-like gorilla hangs from the top of this commercial building in Tokyo.

name: Kintai Bridge
description: One of Japan's most famous sights, the bridge was originally  built in 1673.

name: Ichimonjiya Wasuke
description: Established over a thousand years ago, this family-run teahouse continues to serve skewers of sweet rice cake to this day.

name: Mushizuka at Kan'ei-ji Temple
description: A Buddhist temple to honor insects that died for science.

name: Koukokuji Buddhist Temple
description: Inside the columbarium, thousands of colorful, high-tech Buddhas guard the ashes of the dead.

name: Hiroshima's Hypocenter
description: A plaque marks the site directly below the mid-air detonation of the atomic bomb over Hiroshima.

name: The Hydrangea Train
description: Coast along one of Japan’s mountain railways for coveted views of magnificent hydrangeas.

name: Okishima Island
description: A Japanese cat island also populated by the descendants of a band of banished samurai.

name: Okazaki Shrine
description: The cutest shrine in Kyoto is dedicated to bunnies.

name: Museum of Package Culture
description: Discover the past, present, and future of packaging in this unique Japanese museum.

name: Shakaden Reiyukai Temple
description: Starship-temple contains 400 tons of drinking water and offers free Japanese lessons.

name: Honke Owariya
description: One of Kyoto's oldest food establishments has supplied soba noodles to temple priests and the Imperial Palace.

name: Ashizuri Underwater Observation Tower
description: This retro underwater observation tower would make the perfect lair for a sea-loving villain.

name: Owakudani Black Eggs
description: Legend holds eating just one of these black eggs can add seven years to one's life.

name: Atami Adult Museum
description: Museum showcases Japanese sexuality and some amazing ocean views.

name: Shitamachi Museum
description: A museum dedicated to the history of Tokyo's often-overlooked working-class district.

name: The Great Buddha of Kamakura
description: A bronze statue dating back as far as 1252, still has remnants of gold on its right cheek from ancient gilding.

name: Takeshima Fantasy Museum
description: Shells collected from over a hundred different countries line the walls, floors and ceilings of this fantasy museum.

name: Edo-Tokyo Open Air Architectural Museum
description: Thirty architectural gems from the 19th- and early 20th-century Tokyo were restored and relocated to this space.

name: Shibazakura Park
description: Japan's largest area of "pink moss" turns the hillside bright pink every spring.

name: Kawazu Sakura Festival
description: Cherry blossom season comes early in this Japanese town where pink flowers line the streets and riverbanks.

name: Bennesse Art Site Naoshima
description: A Japanese corporation has turned this small island town into living modern art gallery.

name: Kiyomizu-Dera Temple
description: A shrine contains nearly 200 statues of the Buddhist deity Jizo, dressed in bibs by parents who have lost a child.

name: Izakaya Toyo
description: Blowtorches, tuna, and a jolly, chain-smoking proprietor mark this beloved street-food stand.

name: Intermediatheque
description: An unexpected curiosity cabinet hidden within a department store in the Japan Post tower.

name: Odaiba Statue of Liberty
description: This little replica of Lady Liberty provides a bit of NYC in a Japanese city.

name: Satsuki and Mei's House
description: A real-life replica of a home from Hayao Miyazaki's beloved movie "My Neighbor Totoro."

name: Jigokudani (Hell Valley)
description: A steaming, boiling hellscape sources Hokkaido's thermal baths.

name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial
description: Peace Park and the Atomic Bomb Dome.

name: Shimanami Kaido Bikeway
description: The 43-mile route crosses through six islands with gorgeous views of the Seto Inland Sea.

name: Henn-Na Hotel
description: The world’s first hotel to be run by robots.

name: Rokuon-ji Temple
description: This 14th-century temple is also known as the "Temple of the Golden Pavilion" for its gorgeous gilded reliquary hall.

name: Matsuo Mine
description: Abandoned Japanese ghost town gives a glimpse into what our own metropolises may leave behind.

name: Wind Telephone
description: A disconnected rotary phone for "calling" lost loved ones offered a unique way of dealing with grief in disaster-stricken Japan.

name: Tatami-ishi (Tatami Rocks)
description: Unusual hexagonal rocks formed by cooling lava named for their resemblance to traditional Japanese tatami mats.

name: Kiha
description: It's bad manners to eat and drink on the subways of Tokyo, unless the train is this bar.

name: 'Magokoro Tennyo' ('Goddess of Sincerity')
description: Weighing nearly 7 tons, this massive sculpture was carved out of 500-year-old wood and decorated with thousands of jewels.

name: Kanamara Matsuri Festival
description: A rare festival blending religion, blacksmiths, vagina dentata, sex workers, and the straight-up facts of life.

name: Yasui Kompira-gu Shrine
description: Crawl through the hole in the middle of this stone to break off a bad relationship or initiate a good one.

name: Kanmangafuchi Abyss
description: A remarkable lava formation encloses a line of Buddhist statues that can't be accurately counted, according to local mythology.

name: Kobe Luminarie
description: An astounding annual light display honors the victims of one of Japan's most devastating earthquakes.

name: Okinawa Churaumi Aquarium
description: One of the world's largest tanks mimics the experience of watching rare whale sharks, giant rays, and deep sea creatures from the ocean floor.

name: Aogashima Volcano
description: We heard you like volcanoes so we put a volcano in your volcano so you can die for sure...

name: Nagano's Darkroom
description: This ancient temple is set up to take you on a pilgrimage of self.

name: G-Cans: the World's Largest Drain
description: The world's largest drain sits below the city of Kasukabe, Japan.

name: Tokyo Metropolitan Building Staff Cafeteria
description: The tallest city hall in the world offers an excellent bento box.

name: Fudo Myo-o Statue
description: A moss-covered statue of a Japanese Buddhist deity stands at a quiet temple in downtown Osaka.

name: Asakura Gallery
description: Kawaii meets poisonous at this unique art gallery.

name: Super-Kamiokande
description: Enormous underground chamber built to detect 'ghost particles' of the universe.

name: Transparent Restroom
description: These unique toilets feature transparent glass covered in PDLC film.

name: Suga Shrine Staircase
description: This ordinary set of steps is a must-see destination for fans of a certain romantic anime.

name: Gate Tower Building
description: The Hanshin Expressway goes right through its 5th, 6th, and 7th floors.

name: Museum of Kites
description: This tiny museum hidden above a restaurant houses thousands of modern and traditional kites.

name: Meoto Iwa
description: This pair of small sea rocks has been "wed" using rope and ancient Shinto tradition.

name: Lucky Dragon and Atomic Tuna Memorial
description: An oddly touching memorial to a little-remembered nuclear tragedy.

name: Kameya Kiyonaga
description: This 400-year-old sweet shop serves a delicacy some have described as "edible incense."

name: Tower of the Sun
description: Closed for decades, this strange symbol of Japan's Expo '70 has finally opened its psychedelic insides to the public.

name: Tavern Pachimon Wars
description: This bar is filled with Star Wars merchandise from around the world, all of it unlicensed knockoffs.

name: Japanese Sword Museum
description: This museum showcases the art of ancient sword-making.

name: Mount Nokogiri (Saw Mountain)
description: This sawtooth mountain range still bears the strangely lovely marks of its history as a medieval quarry.

name: The Star Sands of Hoshizuna-no-hama
description: White beach sands made of the remains of tiny, star shaped creatures.

name: Obake Kaidan
description: A flight of this "ghost staircase" has 40 steps on the way up, but only 39 steps on the way down.

name: Mount Aso Caldera
description: The largest volcanic caldera in Japan holds an entire city in its maw.

name: Ningyocho Mechanical Clock Towers
description: When this clock strikes a certain time, puppets showcase a story of life in Edo Japan.

name: Momofuku Ando Instant Ramen Museum
description: An unlikely hit, this museum is dedicated to the inventor of instant ramen and cup noodles.

name: Book and Bed Hostel
description: If you forgot something to read during your trip to Tokyo, this hostel has you covered.

name: Naki Sumo Baby Crying Contest
description: For hundreds of years, participants in this Japanese festival have been making babies cry to ward off demons.

name: Original Nintendo Building
description: The first location of the eventual video game giant.

name: Huis Ten Bosch
description: Historical Japanese-Dutch relations are honored with haunted rides and flower fields in this replica town.

name: Showa Daibutsu
description: The tallest bronze seated statue of the Buddha in Japan.

name: Little Peeing Monk of Tokyo
description: This Japanese Manneken Pis gets new outfits for every occasion.

name: Nabana No Sato
description: These Japanese gardens are blankets of light for dark winter nights.

name: Wara Art
description: Giant animal sculptures are created with the rice straw leftover from the fall harvest each year.

name: Aoyama Technical College
description: A giant robo-cockroach building devours Tokyo.

name: Irugi Shrine
description: A 17th-century Shinto shrine that is home to statues of Mickey Mouse and Pikachu.

name: Tokyu Plaza Rooftop Ferris Wheel
description: The small candy-colored ride is the last rooftop Ferris wheel in Tokyo.

name: The Jade Coast
description: You can still find treasures on the pebbly beaches of one of the world's oldest jade-producing areas.

name: Garden of Unborn Children
description: Rows of stone statues are dressed up to mourn children that are lost before birth.

name: The Sand Museum
description: Giant sand sculptures of famous people and places are on display near the Tottori sand dunes.

name: Shorinzan Darumaji Temple
description: Each year this Japanese temple overflows with scowling, bearded good luck talismans.

name: Chankonabe Shinzan
description: Sidle into a side alley for a taste of sumo wrestlers' traditional hot-pot soup, in a tiny restaurant founded by a former competitor.

name: Mimizuka
description: Burial mound for the ears and noses of thousands of mutilated Korean soldiers.

name: Owl Police Box
description: This adorable birdlike police box is designed based on a play on words.

name: Konagai's Fruit-Shaped Bus Stops
description: This town near Nagasaki is flecked with fruit-flavored bus stops.

name: Secret Cobblestones of Hide-and-Seek Alley
description: A few hidden stones are said to bring fortune and love.

name: Iga Ryu Ninja Museum
description: Be a ninja - or just act like one.

name: Forest of Seven Lucky Gods
description: The divine septet of Japanese fortune gods are rendered in 1,000-year-old wood at this peaceful site.

name: Taiyoshi Hyakuban
description: This brothel-turned-restaurant features plenty of surprises and elaborate, extravagant architecture.

name: Mount Mihara
description: This active volcano's siren song to suicide jumpers forced authorities to build a fence.

name: Nagoro Village
description: As the aging humans of this Japanese village die off one local artisan is replacing them with eerie homonculi.

name: Myth of Tomorrow
description: After being lost for decades, this striking mural depicting the atomic bomb covers the wall of a busy Tokyo metro station.

name: Karuizawa Picture Book Forest Museum
description: Children's literature inside, forest all around.

name: Lake Yamanaka
description: The largest of the Fuji Five Lakes is home to an impressive swan population—some real, some with paddles, and one with an observation deck.

name: Waseda El Dorado
description: This bizarre residential building is the masterpiece of Von Jour Caux, the “Gaudí of Japan.”

name: Tonkararin
description: A mysterious set of tunnels carved in the hills of rural northern Kyushu.

name: Bise Village
description: A network of sandy streets lined with verdant fukugi trees weaves throughout this tiny seaside town.

name: Snow Canyon
description: A massive wintry wall of snow up to 66 feet tall flanks a highway through Japan's mountains.

name: Amazake-chaya Teahouse
description: Existing for more than four centuries, the last of Hakone’s historic teahouses serves a sweet, mold-based rice drink.

name: Atomic Bomb Medical Museum
description: A sobering scientific reminder of the human damage caused by the advent of the nuclear age.

name: Takkoku no Iwaya Bishamondo
description: Founded over a thousand years ago, this shrine is said to be built on the site of a warlord's harem prison.

name: Okunoin Cemetery
description: Largest graveyard in all of Japan surrounds a famous mausoleum.

name: Yawata no Yabushirazu
description: Nestled in an unassuming residential area, this "forbidden forest" is rumored to spirit away those who dare enter it.

name: Naval Underground Headquarters
description: Elaborate series of tunnels where traces of mass suicide are still visible.

name: Maya Hotel
description: An abandoned Art Deco hotel nestled on the side of a wooded mountain.

name: Sample Kobo
description: Make your own fake treats at this factory and workshop in the epicenter of Japan's food replica industry.

name: Kejonuma Leisure Land
description: A quaint amusement park now sits, rusting and forgotten among the foliage.

name: Rollercoaster Bridge (Eshima Ohashi)
description: This bridge is so steep it looks like a rollercoaster.

name: Akiyoshido Cave
description: Approximately 300 million years ago, a massive coral reef existed in what is now the largest limestone cave in Japan.

name: Funaya of Ine
description: The traditional houses along the coast of this charming Japanese fishing village allow people to sleep above the sea.

name: Funaoka Onsen
description: Have a soak at this retro bathhouse, where you will find Japan's first electric bath, tiling from Spain, and wood carvings representing the Japanese invasion of Manchuria.

name: Yamadera Temple Complex
description: The Buddhist temples of Yamadera, built into a mountain side, feature a viewing deck with sweeping views.

name: Kishi Station
description: A calico cat named Tama was the wildly popular Super Station Master at this train stop in southeast Japan.

name: Nihonbashi Kirin Statues
description: Mythical beasts symbolizing the city of Tokyo guard a historic bridge.

name: Meiji University Museum’s Criminal Materials Department
description: Exploring crime and punishment in feudal and pre-modern Japan.

name: Hakubutsukan-Dōbutsuen Station
description: Take a few seconds to look at this abandoned, art-filled station as you pass by on the subway to Ueno.

name: Yokai Yashiki (Monster and Stone Museum)
description: Hidden by the roadside in the Iya Valley, this gem brings together folk monsters and the region's unique geography.

name: Senju Shrine Bomb Shelter
description: One of the few surviving World War II-era air-raid shelters in Tokyo.

name: Abandoned Western Village
description: This western-themed amusement park is host to a large number of decaying animatronic cowboys.

name: Tetsujin 28 Robot Statue
description: A 59-ft. robot statue looks over the citizens of earthquake-ravaged Kobe.

name: Kyotomangekyo Museum
description: Visitors to this museum will discover that kaleidoscopes are much more than toys.

name: Crepe Vending Machine
description: Ever needed a chocolate-filled crepe in the wee hours of the morning?

name: Go'o Shrine
description: Shrine dedicated to boars that is believed to cure foot and leg injuries.

name: Hara Model Railway Museum
description: The largest collection of model trains in the world is actually run using full-size locomotive technology.

name: House of Light
description: This traditional Japanese-style house doubles as one of James Turrell's colorful works of art.

name: Oinusama
description: Rubbing salt on this small stone dog is said to make wishes come true.

name: Tomb of Jesus Christ
description: A small Japanese village claims to be the final resting place of the son of God (and God's other son's ear).

name: Little World Museum of Man
description: An anthropological theme park that depicts the cultures of around 20 different countries.

name: 10-Yen Vending Machines
description: Perhaps the world's cheapest vending machines.

name: Abukuma-do Caves
description: These wondrously illuminated caverns extend under the mountains of the Fukushima Prefecture.

name: Kumamoto Castle
description: The iconic castle that withstood a magnitude 6.2 earthquake has become a symbol of resilience.

name: Buddha's Nostril
description: Devoted Buddhists can earn enlightenment in the next life by crawling through this symbolic nostril.

name: Ribbon Chapel
description: This high-design church is made up of two dizzying spirals meant to evoke the weaving together of marriage.

name: Keihin Industrial Ward
description: An eerie maze of industrial factories looks like something out of a dystopian sci-fi.

name: Human Shadow Etched in Stone
description: An eerie reminder to the horrors of World War II.

name: Tobacco & Salt Museum
description: This museum is dedicated to the cultural history of tobacco and salt, once monopolized in Japan.

name: Okochi Sanso Gardens
description: This garden located inside a bamboo forest once belonged to a famous Japanese actor.

name: Yagiri no Watashi
description: The only Edo-period ferry that is still in operation in Tokyo today.

name: Inakadate Tanbo Art
description: Giant art made in Japanese rice fields.

name: Sendai Daikannon
description: One of the tallest statues in the world, and the tallest statue of a goddess in Japan.

name: The Puchicalator
description: The world's shortest escalator doesn't seem to have any reason to exist other than to hold a world record.

name: Canadian World
description: A semi-abandoned replica of Prince Edward Island's Avonlea—in Japan.

name: 'Coicorin'
description: Tell this pair of "love-inviting" cats in Ginza your romantic hopes.

name: Fukusai-ji Temple
description: The giant figure on the back of this turtle-shaped temple actually hides a memorial pendulum.

name: Washuzan Highland
description: A dilapidated amusement park with a million-dollar view.

name: Tokyo Night & Light Projections
description: The world's largest permanent architectural projection display entertains audiences nightly with 28-story light shows.

name: Nishi-Rokugō Park
description: Watch out for the two-story Godzilla made out of tires.

name: Kamakura's Seven Entrances
description: Narrow passes cut out of mountains surround the medieval capital of Japan.

name: Ajinomoto MSG Factory
description: One of the largest producers of MSG in the world welcomes visitors to its Kawasaki factory with an albino panda named Aji-Kun.

name: Owaraji (Giant Straw Sandals)
description: An enormous pair of straw sandals woven in the traditional way—to keep demons at bay.

name: Ad Museum Tokyo
description: This museum celebrates the history of Japanese advertising, from feudal business tricks to modern T.V. commercials.

name: Mount Osore
description: This mountain's identity is stuck somewhere between a nightmare and a scenic tourist attraction.

name: 'WEB FRAME'
description: Designed by a computer, this intricate network of fluorescent green pipes has taken over a subway station.

name: Taiyo Park
description: A remote and slightly overgrown park filled with replicas of world landmarks.

name: Ancient Orient Museum
description: A hole-in-the-wall antiquities museum in one of Tokyo's most popular megamalls.

name: Ichiriki
description: More than 300 years old, Kyoto's historic "teahouse" still operates as an invitation-only geisha club.

name: Buried Forest Museum
description: Roots and stumps that lay inundated for two millennia are on display in Japan's city of three mysteries.

name: Ushiku Daibutsu: The Great Buddha of Ushiku
description: The third-tallest statue in the world depicts the "Buddha of Infinite Light," complete with observation deck in his chest.

name: Peeing Boy of Iya Valley
description: A statue erected on an infamous cliff where young boys would hold dangerous "pee offs" to prove their mettle.

name: The Asahi Flame
description: This enormous monument is one of the most iconic structures in Tokyo, rudely nicknamed "The Golden Turd."

name: Gakutensoku
description: Japan's first automaton went missing, only to be revived by the Osaka Science Museum.

name: Fugaku Wind Cave
description: One in a pair of subterranean caves created by an ancient eruption of Mount Fuji.

name: Oiteke Bori
description: This water goblin statue represents a 19th-century urban legend about a haunted canal that became a Japanese idiom.

name: Shiotama
description: Steampunk, humanoid sculptures crawl on all fours in a Tokyo plaza.

name: Tin Toy Museum Kitahara Collection
description: More tin toys than you can shake a clapping monkey at.

name: Takaoka Daibutsu: The Great Buddha of Takaoka
description: This giant bronze Buddha, the third largest in Japan, is the last in an ill-fated series of statues.

name: Issen Yoshoku
description: This kitsch-forward diner is dedicated to an almost-forgotten “one-cent” prototype of okonomiyaki.

name: Jinnai Shrine
description: This shrine dedicated to a legendary swordsman has become a pilgrimage site for the ill and infirmed.

name: Chirori the Therapy Dog
description: A touching statue dedicated to Japan's first therapy dog.

name: Silk Road Tarim Uyghur Restaurant
description: Sample specialties from Xinjiang at this Shinjuku neighborhood gem.

name: Monet's Garden Marmottan
description: Nope, not the one in France. The only officially-recognized reproduction of Monet’s garden in Giverny is located in a remote corner of Japan.

name: Izumo-Taisha
description: What's believed to be the country's first shrine is, in Shintō mythology, the annual meeting place of the gods.

name: Chapel Goedele
description: A 14th-century European chapel sticks out of the facade of a Japanese hotel.

name: Daizenji 'Grape Temple'
description: A monk-run winery inside a Buddhist temple celebrates Japan's crown grape varietal.

name: Maishima Incineration Plant
description: This whimsical garbage disposal facility receives around 12,000 accidental tourists each year.

name: Kitano-chō
description: Japan's largest collection of Western-style mansions in the port city of Kobe includes a recreation of Sherlock Holmes' 221B Baker Street quarters.

name: Moyai
description: Inspired by the famous sculptures on Easter Island, this head serves as a popular meeting spot by Shibuya Station.

name: Sarushima - "Monkey Island"
description: A small island with no monkeys but plenty of military ruins.

name: Museum of Tropical Medicine
description: A hub of information on tropical diseases and their treatments.

name: Gunkan Higashi Shinjuku
description: This battleship of a building was designed by a famed "mad architect."

name: Café Paulista
description: Luminaries from Albert Einstein to John Lennon once had a cup of coffee at Tokyo's oldest café.

name: Seikan Tunnel
description: The world's longest tunnel with an undersea section.

name: Yamaboko Festival in Kyoto - Gion Festival

name: Shimizusawa Thermal Power Plant
