name: Rokugeishin
description: A miniature shrine in the busy corner of Tokyo's historic Broadway, honoring a sextet of unusual entertainment gods.

name: Yakuso-Jinshi - Hippocratic Shrine
description: An ancient Greek physician is honored as a god at this hole-in-the-wall shrine.

name: Asakusa Twelve Stories Mural
description: A painting marks the site of a historic skyscraper destroyed by the Great Kantō earthquake.

name: Harakiri Yagura
description: Access to this hidden site of a 14th-century mass suicide is prohibited, unless it is to pray for the dead.

name: Starbucks Dazaifu Tenmangu Omote-Sando
description: This is no ordinary coffee chain outlet—it's an architectural masterpiece.

name: Azabu-Jūban Smile Monuments
description: A series of smile-themed sculptures created by artists from all over the world fill this Tokyo shopping district.

name: Kamuy Kotan
description: A natural wonder with mythological ties to the local Ainu tradition.

name: Sanatorium - Wonder Museum Annex
description: A bizarre, tuberculosis sanatorium-themed café moonlighting as an underground art gallery.

name: Sushi Memorials at Namiyoke Shrine
description: Outside of where Tsukiji Market once stood, these memorials honor the spirits of the fallen fish, shrimp, and eggs used for nigiri.

name: Sampuru Shops of Kappabashi Street
description: Tokyo’s “Kitchen Town” hosts a number of shops selling intricate food replicas.

name: Tokoname Pottery Footpath
description: A mile-long trail of pottery culture, full of clay pipes and lucky cats, including one that’s 12.5 feet tall.

name: Berobero no Kamisama
description: This extremely naked God of Drunkards welcomes visitors to Kochi Station.

name: Poké Lid #100
description: Cute manhole covers commemorate the hometown of the real-life Ash Ketchum who created Pokémon.

name: Ogushi Kaizuka Park
description: A park built around the legend of a clam-loving giant who created a monumental midden.

name: Kölner Aussicht
description: Since Tokyo's Kiyosu Bridge was inspired by a historic bridge that crossed over the Rhine, this viewing spot is known as a "view of Cologne."

name: Kiezu-no-hi (The Eternal Flame)
description: In 806, Kobo Daishi began meditating on Mount Misen. The Buddhist monk lit this holy fire, which has been kept burning for 1,200 years.

name: Sado Kinzan
description: For centuries, this gold mine was the powerhouse behind the Tokugawa Shogunate.

name: Nanzoin Temple
description: A serene mountaintop temple complex featuring one big, bronze Buddha.

name: The Lions of Namiyoke Inari Shrine
description: This shrine, built to protect against destructive waves, is home to two monumental lion heads.

name: Fudarakusan-ji
description: A temple made famous by Japanese Buddhist monks who sacrificed themselves at sea.

name: Kokudō Station
description: Unchanged for decades, World War II-era bullet holes and nostalgic ghost signs still haunt this desolate train station.

name: Hanashi Zuka (Grave of Censored Stories)
description: This “grave” memorializes the 53 rakugo stories that were once banned by the national censorship.

name: Nishinoshima
description: This volcanic island in the Pacific just keeps growing and growing.

name: Ashio Copper Mine
description: The site of Japan's first major environmental disaster.

name: Shin-Nishiarai Octopus Slide
description: Installed in 1965, this whimsical slide has spawned a brood of “octopus mountains” across Japan.

name: Takao Inari Shrine
description: A unique shrine dedicated to the deified skull of a 17th-century Tokyo courtesan.

name: The Fountain of Ideas
description: Ward off bad luck and fill your bottle with "the most watery water" at this little roadside shrine in Yonezawa.

name: R.T.O. Relief
description: A beautiful work of art designed to impress the Allied Forces.

name: Oginoya’s Kettle Rice Pottery
description: This iconic “station bento” comes in an earthenware pot you can take home and repurpose.

name: Ubagaike Pond
description: According to local legend, an ancient serial killer nicknamed the “Hag” once haunted this pond.

name: Muroto Schoolhouse Aquarium
description: Sharks, stingrays, and turtles swim in the pools at this former elementary school.

name: Bone Buddhas at Isshin-ji Temple
description: This temple is home to a group of Buddha statues made from thousands of bones and ashes of the deceased.

name: Hodota Burial Mounds
description: A trio of keyhole-shaped burial mounds restored to their ancient glory.

name: Shōzoku Inari-jinja Shrine
description: Thousands of foxes are said to gather and parade across the neighborhood of Ōji on New Year’s Eve.

name: Cosmo Isle Hakui
description: A museum of space exploration in the UFO capital of Japan.

name: Site of Ganso Nagahama-ya
description: The former site of Fukuoka’s legendary ramen joint works hard to guide customers to the “original” original.

name: Omirokusama Shrine
description: Plagued with warts? Leave a frog at this small shrine in the city of Tosa, and you may be cured.

name: Kimi-Chan
description: This sculpture bears a tragic backstory that may have inspired a hauntingly beautiful children's song.

name: Godzilla Rock
description: A natural resemblance of the monster of film lore.

name: Japon Louvre Sculpture Museum
description: A lesser-known sister to Paris's world-famous museum, located within a Buddhist sculpture paradise.

name: Gallery of Horyuji Treasures
description: This lesser-visited gallery in a corner of the Tokyo National Museum complex is an architectural beauty dedicated to the ancient treasures of Hōryū-ji.

name: Honeybee Drinking Fountain
description: Every summer, this shrine sets up a tiny drinking fountain reserved for thirsty apians.

name: 'The Next Step'
description: A museum in Hokkaido is housed in a former high school. On its grounds a marker shares the awakening of a man who longs to be reunited with his soulmate in the fictional world of Kcymaerxthaere.

name: TOTO Toilet Museum
description: This futuristic-looking museum was built for the centennial of Japan's most innovative toilet.

name: Tomb of the Nue
description: The tomb of an infamous chimeric monster whose corpse once washed ashore in Osaka.

name: Yasaka Kōshin-dō Temple
description: This compact temple is filled with garlands of colorful balls that represent restrained monkeys.

name: Tomizawa Site (Underground Forest Museum)
description: A 20,000-year-old fossilized forest is preserved at this unique museum.

name: Shibarare Jizoson
description: A bizarre object of worship believed to grant wishes to those who tie it up with a rope.

name: Mito Low District Water Tower
description: This beautiful defunct water tower adds a European flair to a traditional Japanese neighborhood.

name: Kamo Aquarium
description: Marvel at the world's largest collection of jellyfish, including more than 10,000 undulating moon jellies.

name: Usami Kannon-ji
description: The largest seated Buddha statue in Japan is the centerpiece of an abandoned complex in a quiet, seaside town.

name: Five Hundred Arhats
description: Hundreds of statues of Buddha’s disciples meditating, napping, sneezing, and picking their noses.

name: Nana-chan Mannequin
description: This giant fashion model is one of the most beloved symbols of Nagoya.

name: Tokyo Metropolitan Medicinal Botanical Garden
description: Home to a garden of poisonous plants and the only legal cannabis and opium farms in Tokyo.

name: Five Zelkovas of Kami-Itabashi
description: Various accidents and illnesses fell upon the developers who tried to remove these historic trees.

name: Fugoppe Cave Petroglyphs
description: One of the only two archaeological sites in Japan to feature petroglyphs, which some believe to be a long-lost writing system of the gods.

name: Tokyo German Village
description: This theme park is not in Tokyo, not especially German, and certainly not a village.

name: Suribachiyama Tumulus
description: Unbeknownst to many, an ancient burial mound hides in plain sight in Ueno Park.

name: ‘Tora-san and His Sister’
description: Life-sized statues of two characters from a decades-long Japanese film series stand outside Shibamata station.

name: Mandarado Burial Grottoes
description: An eerie cluster of medieval cave-like tombs carved out of a cliff, hidden in a historic mountain pass.

name: Thousand-Armed Insect Goddess of Mercy
description: A bizarre statue of a Buddhist goddess made from 20,000 insects.

name: Nyanya Magari Cat Alley
description: A hidden alley with seven corners and handmade cat decorations.

name: Otaru Orgel Museum
description: This dazzling three-story shop showcases the art and history of music boxes, a popular novelty in Japan.

name: Nekodan (Cat Stairway)
description: According to local legend, anyone who falls on these temple steps will turn into a cat.

name: Hanshin Expressway Earthquake Museum
description: A museum run by retired highway engineers who helped rebuild after the Great Hanshin Earthquake.

name: Hirosaki Castle
description: The northernmost of the 12 surviving feudal castles of Japan is known as a cherry blossom viewing spot.

name: Orizuru Wall
description: This glass wall is gradually filling up with paper cranes, in honor of Hiroshima’s past and dedication to its future.

name: Ganki Inari
description: A narrow, dead-end alley hides with a small fox shrine and the untold history of Yokohama's historic red-light district.

name: Shoden Island
description: Overlooked by many, this tiny sacred island in Ueno Park only opens to the public every 12 days.

name: Lake Imba Monster
description: The strange “kaiju” of Lake Imba that allegedly killed over a dozen men with a single strike of lightning.

name: Valley of Gangala
description: Once a huge limestone cave that collapsed thousands of years ago, this scenic ravine is also an important archaeological site.

name: Basho Memorial Statue
description: Once the park closes at sunset, the greatest haiku poet of all time moves to face the Sumida River.

name: Sahara Glass Park
description: This glass-themed tourist park in northern Japan feels like taking a step back in time.

name: Minato City Local History Museum
description: A museum housed in a stunning Rockefeller-funded medical institute building from the 1930s.

name: Odoriba Station
description: This subway station is named after a local legend about supernatural dancing cats.

name: Shinagawa Station
description: Japan's first train station is also the site of Godzilla's first destructive visit to the nation.

name: Hotei Buddha
description: The largest privately owned statue of Buddha in Japan, built by a total amateur, with an unconventional look.

name: Mōka Station
description: Looking for the train station? Try the building shaped like a train.

name: Muryōju-in Temple Site
description: Home to 800-year-old zen basin, a 400-year-old pilgrim’s path, and a century-old industrial grotto.

name: Kankōji Temple
description: Take a lick of the legendary homemade miso paste that protected this Buddhist temple’s scrolls from a fire centuries ago.

name: Usuki Stone Buddhas
description: These medieval stone-cut Buddhist statues may protect visitors from being fired.

name: Kanda Myojin Shrine
description: You can receive a blessing for your electronics at this Shinto shrine near Akihabara.

name: The Zodiac Gods of Horikiri
description: All 12 animals of the Chinese zodiac are depicted in anthropomorphized deity form.

name: Mitaki-dera
description: A mountaintop Buddhist temple with panoramic views of Hiroshima.

name: Tsuburaya Eiji Museum
description: A small, family-friendly museum dedicated to the “god of special effects” who brought Godzilla to life.

name: Hansobo Shrine
description: A mountaintop shrine-temple guarded by a horde of half-demon priests.

name: Miyoshi Mononoke Museum
description: A collection of nearly 5,000 yokai-themed toys, sculptures, and more, dating from the 1700s to the present day.

name: Witch Hat Intake Tower
description: An adorable intake tower with a “witch hat” stands in the Edo River.

name: Monozuki Cafe
description: A tiny kissaten coffeeshop with expertly crafted coffee in an antique setting.

name: Upopoy (National Ainu Museum and Park)
description: Learn about the indigenous people of Japan's northernmost islands.

name: Mount Hiyori
description: Officially the lowest mountain in Japan.

name: Ariake Incineration Plant Chimney Clock
description: The enigmatic symbol on this incineration plant's smokestack is, in fact, a one-of-a-kind clock.

name: Genbudo Park
description: A dramatic landscape of ancient lava, carved into hexagonal columns by nature itself.

name: Yubizuka
description: This surreal thumbs-up monument commemorates a shiatsu legend.

name: Yokohama Doll Museum
description: Home to thousands of dolls from around the world, including the "blue-eyed dolls" that came to Japan as American ambassadors.

name: Showa Retro Packaging Museum
description: A collection of nostalgic items from the Showa era with an exhibit on the local ghost story of the Snow Woman.

name: Nipro Hachiko Dome
description: This sports stadium was made from 25,000 cypress trees and ranks among the world's largest wooden domes.

name: Kuragari Pass
description: This quaint mountain pass with centuries-old cobblestones is known as Japan's steepest slope.

name: Tsukiji Hongan-ji Temple
description: A grand Buddhist temple with Indian influences.

name: Site of the Rokumeikan
description: Here once stood an opulent state guest house that symbolized the Westernization of Japan.

name: Yayoi Archaeological Site
description: The original location of the eponymous site of Japan’s Yayoi period is now lost in time.

name: Mahō Jinja (Magical Shrine)
description: This unusual "magical shrine" honors a legendary raccoon-dog god that stowed away to Japan from some far-off land.

name: Yohashira-jinja Shrine
description: This traditional Japanese shrine is dedicated to four Shinto deities.

name: Kiyosu Castle
description: This reconstructed castle was once the base of the “Devil King” who almost united the feudal warring states.

name: ‘Insect Mound’
description: Nestled among the bamboos in a temple complex, this art installation serves as a unique memorial to insects.

name: Hideyo Noguchi Statue
description: This site commemorates the life of a Japanese scientist who dedicated his life to the discovery of diseases.

name: Ame no Sakahoko
description: This mythical weapon stuck on the mountaintop is also the site of the first honeymoon in Japan's history.

name: Hao-Hao Tunnel
description: Over 80 years old, this hand-dug tunnel is a Chinese-style portal to another world.

name: The Big Namahage Statues
description: Two ogres stand guard at the Oga Visitor Center.

name: Shibu Onsen
description: Nine healing baths available only to those who stay in this quaint town, said to have inspired 'Spirited Away.'

name: Yatozaka Kiridoshi Pass
description: An ancient mountain-cut pass runs largely forgotten in the suburbs of Kamakura.

name: Akita Dog Visitor Center
description: An Akita-themed facility modeled after the old Shibuya Station, erected in honor of Hachiko, a famously loyal dog.

name: Nissan Heritage Collection
description: This former factory contains an amazing collection of around 400 mint-condition Nissan cars.

name: Inshallah
description: For half a century, this Arabian-inspired retro café has been an oasis for locals in Okinawa’s central tourist area.

name: Mount Ishimaki
description: A long-worshipped mountain, considered a "power spot" and suspected to be an ancient pyramid by some.

name: Ghost Chimney Monument
description: Once known for its eerie appearance, this semicircular monument is all that remains of a Tokyo industrial icon.

name: Rising Sun Revolving Door
description: At the time of its construction, this type of door was nearly unheard of.

name: Hosoya’s Sandwich
description: Established in 1950, this humble burger joint is the oldest surviving one in Japan.

name: Thirty-Three Avatars of Kannon
description: In the former treasury of a popular temple, a rare sculptural depiction of Kannon’s diverse avatars.

name: Ryuko-Myojin Shrine
description: Once upon a time in Kamakura, a five-headed dragon fell in love with an Indian water goddess.

name: Memorial Park of Houses Destroyed by Debris Flows
description: Buried nine feet under, these houses are preserved as a stark reminder of the disastrous 1991 Unzen eruption.

name: Royal Theater
description: Retro as can be, this cinema still uses film reels and screens revivals of Shōwa-era hits and classics.

name: Watari-Kashiwabara's Pylon 24
description: The only electricity pylon built over two lanes of traffic.

name: Ōfuna Kannon
description: A giant statue of the Buddhist goddess of mercy watches over the town of Ōfuna.

name: 108 Grotto Tombs
description: Hike to a hidden medieval troglodyte necropolis.

name: Noge-Miyakobashi Shotengai (Harmonica Alley)
description: This retro izakaya street is a unique legacy of the 1964 Tokyo Olympics.

name: I-Link Town Observation Deck
description: Just outside Tokyo, a 45-story-high observatory with a stunning view of Tokyo Skytree and Mount Fuji.

name: Onihei Jokei “Historical” Markers
description: “Historical” markers commemorating the sites of fictional events depicted in a popular jidaigeki novel series.

name: Ōmori Nori Vending Machine
description: A vending machine dedicated to a beloved seaweed.

name: Moto-Hakone Stone Buddhas
description: A group of medieval rock-cut Buddhist statues created to save people from the volcanic hell that surrounds them.

name: Umeda Kitamuki Jizo
description: A rare north-facing Ksitigarbha statue enshrined in a passageway off Osaka’s busiest area.

name: Nishi-Ōyama Station
description: Japan Railways' southernmost train station offers a view of a volcano and a mailbox said to grant happiness.

name: Mito Daiichi High School
description: An old-fashioned school with interesting grounds and deep history, including the original Mito Castle gate.

name: Shōsōin
description: A raised Japanese building has preserved 8th-century artifacts for more than a millennium, earning this log cabin a reputation as the "world's oldest museum."

name: Sebayashi Ripple Marks
description: Fifty-odd dinosaur footprints are preserved on this strip of mountain cliff along with ancient ripple marks.

name: Gumbo Hut Shioya
description: Enjoy traditional Louisiana-style gumbo and the spirit of Southern hospitality 7,000 miles from New Orleans.

name: Jūroku Rakan Iwa
description: Buddha figures are carved into volcanic rocks on the seashore of Yamagara Prefecture.

name: Motomiya Movie Theater
description: A disused, crumbling 110-year-old theater retains the authentic retro charms of Showa-era cinema.

name: Bashamichi Station Concourse
description: Remnants of an old bank adorn this station's brick wall.

name: Kifune Shrine
description: This ancient, tranquil shrine is the birthplace of ema prayer boards and the Ushi-no-toki-mairi curse ritual.

name: Billiken-san
description: Up on the iconic Tsutenkaku Tower sits an American mascot that became Osaka’s own lucky god.

name: ‘Homesick Hachiko’
description: Another statue of Japan's most famous dog stands in his hometown.

name: Tanuki Street
description: A tiny street lined with eleven distinct Tanuki statues, each representing a different wish.

name: Maria of the Ant Village
description: This church commemorates a venerable, yet nigh-forgotten woman who dedicated her life to the poor in post-war Tokyo.

name: Shima Spain Village
description: This Spanish theme park stands out in the Japanese countryside.

name: Takikawa Sky Park
description: Pilots from around the world come to this park to fly gliders.

name: Myoken-ji
description: A relatively young Buddhist temple on hilltop, dedicated to a statue relocated from Mount Atago.

name: Tomb of the Wet Kimono
description: The birthplace of the Japanese idiom "wet clothes" meaning “false accusation."

name: Arima Family Plot
description: No one knows why this family’s graves stayed on the national museum's premises after all other tombstones were relocated.

name: Tomori Stone Lion
description: The oldest stone lion in Okinawa survived a major battle during World War II.

name: Shōfukuji Temple Jizōdō Hall
description: Considered the oldest intact wooden structure in Tokyo Prefecture, this impressive temple was built in 1407.

name: Tobinodai Shell Midden
description: The remains of a prehistoric settlement with a museum showcasing fascinating archaeological finds next to modern artworks.

name: Tarumizu Sanctuary
description: A sacred place for ascetic training, set in a honeycomb-walled cliffside cave deep in the woods.

name: Ikebukuro Shimento Pagoda
description: Dedicated to the victims of a phantom slasher, this memorial is claimed by some to be the source of a deadly curse.

name: Asukayama Park
description: Visit the cherry blossoms at Tokyo’s unofficial smallest mountain.

name: Tarumizu Sanctuary
description: A sacred place for ascetic training, set in a honeycomb-walled cliffside cave deep in the woods.

name: Nyan-nyan-ji
description: A century-old house transformed into a cat temple by an artist.

name: Ikebukuro Shimento Pagoda
description: Dedicated to the victims of a phantom slasher, this memorial is claimed by some to be the source of a deadly curse.

name: Yamanoue Stele and Tumulus
description: The oldest surviving stele in Japan is a personal dedication from a monk to his late mother.

name: Deai-sugi Cedar Tree
description: This train station meeting spot is an actual, millennia-old tree from the island of Yakushima.

name: Tomioka Silk Mill
description: The World Heritage-designated site of Japan’s industrial revolution, once mistaken as a den of vampires.

name: Yamataka Jindai Zakura
description: One of Japan’s oldest and largest cherry trees is said to have been planted by an ancient, heroic prince.

name: None Kitchen
description: Kokera sushi may be the rarest sushi you have never heard of.

name: Urashima-Kannon Temple
description: Associated with one of Japan's most famous fairy tales, this temple is home to a statue purportedly brought from the undersea palace of the Dragon God.

name: Hōjō-Komachi Archaeological Remains
description: The preserved ruins of the Hōjō clan’s palatial estate, hidden in the basement of a tourist information center.

name: Nintendo Headquarters
description: The home of Mario and his friends

name: ‘Future City Galactic Earth Railway’
description: A fantastical glow-in-the-dark mural inspired by the city’s most famous writer, Kenji Miyazawa.

name: Taro Okamoto Memorial Museum
description: The home and studio of one of Japan's most avant-garde artists is now a museum showcasing his surreal works.

name: Nusuttogari
description: Off the beaten path along the coastline of Miura, a scenic cliff with an evocative name.

name: Kappabuchi Pool
description: Visitors are encouraged to fish for water goblins at this haunted pool.

name: Gojōban Yashiki Samurai Residences
description: Descendants of the samurai who guarded Matsusaka Castle during the Edo period still live in these charming historic houses (and visitors can enter one of them for free).

name: Friendship Tree Duet Phone
description: A rare double-receiver payphone housed in a fake tree.

name: Nakamura Keith Haring Collection
description: The only museum dedicated to the American street artist is in rural Japan.

name: Egg Sandwich Laboratory Vending Machine
description: Open 24 hours, this self-serve sandwich spot specializes in egg salad sandwiches in a land that takes them very seriously.

name: Prince Morinaga's Dungeon
description: The alleged site of the months-long imprisonment of a Japanese prince.

name: Kinomoto Tsuruya Bakery
description: This bakery in Japan is one of the few places that still sells 'salad bread,' a short-lived staple in the post-World War II years.

name: Kigakurin Banshōji Temple
description: In a shopping arcade, a modernized temple deeply tied to feudal Japan’s prominent warlords.

name: Namazu-San, the Lucky Catfish
description: Thanks to wordplay, the earthquake-causing catfish monster has become a god of confidence at this shrine.

name: Grave of Princess Centipede
description: Legend says that a swarm of centipedes and snakes once crawled out of the tomb of this cursed princess.

name: Jinde
description: A mysterious, perhaps haunted plot of land, untouched by modern development.

name: Meganenohi (Monument to Eyeglasses)
description: A replica of an ancient alien-like idol humorously represents spectacles in a corner of this shrine complex.

name: Mimeguri Shrine
description: This shrine is home to an unusual trifold torii and a replica Trafalgar Square lion.

name: Hall of the Ten Kings at Ennoji Temple
description: Feast your eyes on the centuries-old statues of the judges of hell.

name: Amazake-babaa
description: This statue of a deified “hag” is said to protect children from the common cold and coughing fits.

name: Kofuku Train Station
description: This station on an abandoned railway line, named for “happiness,” is still a popular destination for lovers.

name: Nishiki Secret Base Museum
description: Secret tunnels leading to underground facilities lurk near the remains of a World War II-era Japanese Navy airbase.

name: Tsuzuki Mamoru-kun
description: A giant gorilla guardian of the neighborhood, warning people to drive safely.

name: Confucius Shrine in Nagasaki
description: This ornate example of 19th-century Chinese architecture is one of only a handful of Confucius shrines in Japan.

name: Mawaki Site
description: The site of a prehistoric settlement spanning millennia, complete with a reconstructed timber circle.

name: Old Sogenji Temple Gate
description: All that remains of Okinawa's royal temple are a restored gateway and a tall banyan tree.

name: Higashi-Fushimi Inari Shrine
description: The lesser-known eastern counterpart to Kyoto’s famous fox shrine.

name: Faret Tachikawa
description: The former site of the U.S. military base redeveloped as an art district, boasting a collection of 109 public artworks from 36 countries.

name: Ryuren no Kane
description: A romantic escape inspired by the legend of a child-eating five-headed dragon.

name: Demon’s Hand Prints in the Rocks
description: According to local folklore, a demon left his handprints on these boulders, which became the prefecture’s namesake.

name: Weird Museum for Boys and Girls
description: Fanatical collections of vintage toys, dolls, clothes, and objects.

name: Nishiki Tenmangū Shrine
description: A 1000-year-old shrine in Kyoto with a poorly planned sacred arch.

name: Haunted Acala Stele of Moriya
description: For but one summer in 1975, this rural suburb became a busy tourist spot when a local girl claimed to have seen ghostly faces on an old stele.

name: First Flight Monument
description: A monument marking the first airplane flights in Japan hints at the dark history behind its peaceful park setting.

name: Mujinazuka at Kenshō-ji
description: A legend of a mysterious phantom train haunts this grave.

name: Otowa Bridge
description: A peaceful place to spot the rare and iconic red-crowned crane.

name: Maboroshi Hakurankai
description: A bizarre assault of surreal art hiding in bushes and huts on a hill.

name: Shiozawa Tsumugi Fabric Museum
description: Weave yourself into over a thousand years of history at this textiles museum through demonstrations and a hands-on hataori experience on a traditional loom.

name: Tozan Shrine
description: A whimsical shrine with a porcelain Torii gate, split in half by an active train line.

name: Shinodanomori Shrine
description: A mystical shrine dedicated to a forest that once covered the area and the legendary fox spirit who lived there.

name: Kifune Castle
description: An eccentric castle with snake protectors and spectacular views of Beppu.

name: Tsuribune Shrine
description: At the "fishing boat shrine," an offering is said to help ward off fevers.

name: Sapporo Noah’s Ark
description: This izakaya restaurant was designed to look like the Biblical vessel.

name: Dorikono Slope
description: A slope with a mysterious name recalls a discontinued pre-war energy drink.

name: Iwakiyama Shrine
description: An atmospheric shrine of mountain worship that is home to a number of curious folk tales.

name: Nanbu Town Dragon Tower
description: Scale the heights of this 100-foot play space shaped like a ferocious dragon.

name: Dofuchi Strait
description: The world's narrowest strait is often mistaken for a canal.

name: Grave of Yasujirō Ozu
description: The legendary Japanese filmmaker’s tombstone is marked by a single letter meaning “nothing," and bottles of sake.

name: Hikō Jinja—Shrine of Flight
description: Dedicated to humankind's achievements in aviation, this Shintō shrine is notable for its Greek-style architecture and collection of aircrafts.

name: Enkiri Enoki
description: A small shrine run entirely by vending machines.

name: Uchimura Kanzo Memorial Stone Church
description: An unusual structure of stone and glass honoring one of Japan’s most eminent Christian leaders who founded the non-church movement.

name: 730 Crossing
description: This monumental intersection commemorates the day Okinawa switched to driving on the left.

name: Aeon Mall Okinawa Rycom Aquarium
description: Located in the large-scale shopping mall is an otherwise unassuming aquarium featuring species native to Okinawa waters.

name: Shin-Ōhashi Bridge
description: The predecessor to this bridge was depicted in a famous Japanese woodcut print that influenced Van Gogh.

name: National Astronomy Observatory Japan Mitaka Campus
description: The headquarters of one of Japan’s leading astronomy research institutions features several historic observatory facilities and a 2,000-year-old burial mound.

name: Stone God of Nanoka-Ichiba
description: Considered an enigma by folklorists, an object of primitive animistic worship lives on in Yamanashi.

name: Port of Kobe Earthquake Memorial Park
description: This massive section of quay destroyed by an earthquake is preserved to remember the past.

name: Namida-ishi
description: A single block in this stone staircase to the temple is mysteriously always wet.

name: Tekona Reijindo
description: This one-of-a-kind shrine is dedicated to a girl who was too beautiful for her own good.

name: Ultraman Street
description: In the hometown of Eiji Tsuburaya, a street lined with intergalactic heroes and monsters.

name: Forbidden Forest at Keta Taisha Shrine
description: This “forbidden” sanctuary has managed to preserve a unique, ancient ecosystem.

name: Former Ozu Residence
description: The former mansion complex of a merchant prince, related to a famed filmmaker.

name: Okehazama Battlefield
description: The location of the site of one of the defining battles of feudal Japan is being fought over by two neighboring cities.

name: Former Temiya Line Railroad
description: This abandoned railroad got a new life as a scenic promenade cutting through the city of Otaru.

name: Sarcophagal Amitabha-Buddha
description: Though not uncommon in western Japan, this Buddha cut out of an ancient sarcophagus is the only example in central Tokyo.

name: Kasori Shell Mounds
description: This prehistoric site is home to the largest shell midden cluster in Japan.

name: Night-Crying Sumo Wrestlers
description: A quartet of temple guardian statues that allegedly cry at night for being forbidden from wrestling.

name: Yamashita Archaeological Cave
description: Archaeologists continue to debate whether this site yielded the oldest human remains in Japan.

name: Ochobamba
description: An old woman cured cholera in the 1820s and now her grave is said to be the city’s most cursed object.

name: Sapporo Clock Tower
description: Japan’s oldest clock tower has been called one of the country’s most disappointing attractions.

name: Winding Tower of Shime Coal Mine
description: Japan’s only surviving winding tower.

name: Umanose Cave Mouth
description: Once an erosional cave, this scenic arch was lifted up above the sea's surface by an earthquake.

name: Kasane-ga-fuchi
description: The eerie, tragic setting of one of Japan’s most popular ghost stories.

name: Imashirozuka Kofun
description: The long-lost tomb of an ancient Japanese emperor, complete with a miniature terracotta army.

name: Aburaya Kumahachi Sculpture
description: This statue of the "shiny uncle of Beppu" was erected in 2007.

name: Dr. Hepburn’s Former Residence
description: The man who created the first Japanese-English Dictionary.

name: Shiogama Seafood Wholesale Market
description: One of the last great old-school fish markets in Japan's Tohoku Region.

name: Dejima Island
description: A faithful reconstruction of what was once the only window into Japan.

name: Japan's Biggest Statue of Liberty
description: This replica of the Statue of Liberty is a quarter of the size of the real thing. It sits on the same latitude as the original, but on the other side of the world.

name: Dagashiya Game Museum
description: This time capsule lets you enjoy decades-old Japanese arcade games.

name: Edison Bamboo
description: The bamboo that helped Thomas Edison develop a longlasting lightbulb.

name: Prince Pujie’s Honeymoon House
description: This house was once the residence of Aisin-Gioro Pujie, the younger brother of China’s last emperor.

name: Tatsunokuchi Execution Grounds
description: A major Japanese execution ground site from the Middle Ages.

name: Six Ksitigarbhas of Yuigahama
description: These statues are dedicated to the damned souls of those executed centuries ago on the beautiful Yuigahama beach.

name: Rock-Splitting Cherry Tree
description: A 360-year-old cherry tree grows out of a granite boulder.

name: Kamegaoka Stone Age Site
description: This prehistoric burial site is where one of Japan’s most iconic ancient idols was found.

name: Gonokami Maimaizu Well
description: Originally built circa 13-14th century, this “snail well” was a resourceful means to draw water in a region where the soil was mostly loam.

name: Funato Kappa Park
description: A small park inhabited by handmade water goblins.

name: Zenringai (Zen Temple Street)
description: These 33 Zen Buddhist temples were built to protect the nearby castle.

name: ‘Shake Hands With Lima-chan’
description: Commemorating the Japanese migration to Peru, this statue has a sister standing across the Pacific.

name: Muchachos
description: A hidden empanada shop run by the biggest Boca Juniors F.C Japanese fan.

name: Seimei Stone
description: Associated with Japanese folklore's most eminent sorcerer, this slab of rock is said to be both a blessing and a curse.

name: Okinawa Prefectural Peace Memorial Park
description: A unique memorial made up of a collection of memorials, all commemorating the Battle of Okinawa in World War II.

name: Suzuhiro Kamaboko Museum
description: Dedicated to the history and culture of Japanese fishcake, this museum invites you to take part in its kamaboko-making workshop.

name: 13 Bridges of the Ebi River
description: A bronze ship jutting out of a bridge, a commemoration of Osamu Dazai, a sculpture created by 30 manga legends, and more.

name: Kurozuka (Black Tomb)
description: Local legend claims that this is the final resting place of an infamous cannibal hag.

name: Jo-Jo-style Bar Dio
description: A bar dedicated to he Jojo manga franchise, with over 50 original cocktails.

name: Site of the Namamugi Incident
description: After an Englishmen was killed by a samurai here in 1862, a two-day war changed the course of Japanese history.

name: Otaru Herring Palace
description: A nouveau-riche hilltop “palace” that once provided lodging to more than a hundred fishermen.

name: Mamoru-kun Patrolman
description: Made to improve road safety, these policemen-like figurines in Miyakojima island have earned an enthusiastic fanbase.

name: Kashikobuchi Spider Stele
description: A long-forgotten homage to an eldritch, sassy spider yōkai that was once worshipped here as a god.

name: Ubayama Shell Mound
description: At this Neolithic site, the remains of ancient humans have been found alongside the bones of the toxic fugu pufferfish.

name: Himeji Castle Shrine
description: A displaced shrine that was put back after a string of bad luck.

name: Kanibōzu Claw Marks
description: A giant man-eating crab monster allegedly left its claw marks on this unassuming boulder.

name: Tone Canal Billiken Shrine
description: An American mascot that became a lucky god in Japan, enshrined in a city far from the main center of his worship.

name: Terranova Music Box Museum
description: Hokkaido's largest music box museum, featuring music boxes that are unique in Japan and the world.

name: Organic Building
description: An office building covered from head to toe in over eighty variety of plants

name: Yomotsu-Hirasaka
description: The sacred slope said to mark the gateway to the underworld.

name: Seiryu Miharashi Station
description: This train station has no entrances or exits for pedestrians, and exists only to take advantage of the area’s view.

name: Sagami River Bridge Ruins
description: These medieval wooden bridge pillars appeared out of nowhere in the aftermath of a huge earthquake.

name: Yushima Tenmangū
description: Students pray here in hopes of entering one of Japan’s most prestigious universities.

name: Tengu Stelae of Naruse
description: Three rare “road guardian” stelae with reliefs of the Tengu can be found in the town of Naruse.

name: Ota Shrine
description: Reaching Japan’s “most dangerous shrine” means climbing a vertical cliff.

name: Masked God Boze of Akusekijima Island
description: Once a year, these 'visitor deities' chase away evil spirits with a phallic-shaped staff.

name: Jimesaa
description: Locals have put a new makeup on this strange statue every year since they unearthed it.

name: Kuwabara Thunder Well
description: Electricians pray for luck at this place where, according to legend, the god of thunder was once trapped.

name: Juchheim
description: This Western confectionery in Kobe made the German tree stump cake into a Japanese staple.

name: Honnō-ji Temple
description: The infamous 'Incident at Honnō-ji' brought an end to Oda Nobunaga’s attempt to unify a warring Japan.

name: ‘Portrait of a Shout’
description: A well-known Japanese musician’s all-night concert on this island is immortalized by an unusual work of art.

name: Kadonowaki Elementary School Ruins
description: A school destroyed by fire and tsunami simultaneously is preserved as a memorial.

name: ‘Portrait of a Shout’
description: A well-known Japanese musician’s all-night concert on this island is immortalized by an unusual work of art.

name: Communal Grave of Kochibara and Akahigibara Clans
description: Thousands of members of one family rest in peace at Okinawa's largest community gravesite.

name: National Film Archive Permanent Exhibition
description: An exhibit tracing Japan's cinematic history in an often-overlooked national museum.

name: Mōtsū-ji
description: This Buddhist temple may have the best example of a Pure Land garden in Japan.

name: Demon Oak Tree of Onizawa
description: A mountain demon once helped a local villager construct a canal, after which he sat on this oak tree to rest.

name: Yokohama Customs “Queen’s Tower”
description: The Port of Yokohama’s customs headquarters building resembles a Moorish kasbah, complete with a minaret.

name: Grave of Akira Kurosawa
description: Japan's legendary filmmaker is interred in a small graveyard in a similarly modest temple in Kamakura.

name: Tatsuoka Castle Site
description: The site of one of the only two star-shaped forts in Japan was used as an elementary school for over a century, much longer than its life as a castle.

name: Naramachi Nepalese Window
description: A traditional Nepalese window blends into the historic merchant district of Old Nara Town.

name: Site of the Golden Dragon Spring
description: An unassuming set of tiles next to a mailbox mark the former location of one of the most famous springs in the city.

name: Tozawa Goryeo Hall
description: A roadside stop offers a taste of Korean culture.

name: Sanada no Osada
description: According to Japanese mythology, the first rice paddy in history was created here many aeons ago.

name: Nobunaga Daimyōjin
description: A Japanese fertility god in the shape of a large phallus.

name: Ju-oh-iwa
description: A rock with images of infernal judges sculpted on it, offering a panoramic view of Kamakura.

name: Minatogawa Fissure
description: The remains of the Minatogawa Man found here are among the oldest known skeleton specimens from Japan.

name: Kajiki Unofficial Mint Site
description: Imitations of Chinese coins were minted here for foreign trade before Japan got its own currency.

name: Chonmage-zuka
description: The burial site of 16 men’s hair buns, which were lopped off in place of their heads.

name: Futago Park
description: This park was once home to now-extinct Naumann's elephants, and what might have been a real Banksy.

name: Kamakura Carnival Mural
description: This frenetic mural recalls an annual festival that once took the city by storm.

name: ‘Benzaiten and the Women of the World’
description: An eclectic fountain commemorating the 1964 Summer Olympics.

name: Great Well of Ōme-Shinmachi
description: The largest example of the “snail well” unique to the Musashino Plateau region.

name: The Yellow Handkerchief of Happiness
description: The climactic location of the 1977 film 'The Yellow Handkerchief'  commemorates its concept of happiness.

name: Indian Drinking Fountain
description: A memorial to the Indian victims of the devastating 1923 earthquake, built in glorious Mughal style.

name: Kaiten Memorial Museum
description: This museum tells the stories of Japanese soldiers who went on torpedo kamikaze missions.

name: Wakae Island Ruins
description: The ruins of the oldest artificial island in Japan, still used for mooring today at 800 years old.

name: Kaname-ishi
description: At this shrine, a sacred stone is said to keep a sea monster at bay.

name: Grave of Benkei
description: According to folklore, this grave lies near the site of the “standing death” of one of Japan’s fiercest warriors.

name: Fudai Floodgate
description: Once considered a massive folly, this "miracle floodgate" saved its town from tsunami devastation in 2011.

name: Shōchiku Ōfuna Studio Site
description: Iconic Japanese movies were made at this forgotten film-studio-site-turned-mall.

name: Kateki: Erasmus from the De Liefde Shipwreck
description: This historic Erasmus statue was first mistaken for a legendary Chinese inventor, then as a child-eating hag.

name: Nekonoike Tunnel
description: This unassuming tunnel’s name recalls a storied "cat pond" that has now been lost.

name: Tokiwa-sō Manga Museum
description: A replica of the boarding house where many pioneering mangaka lived in the 1950s and ’60s.

name: Nozokibashi Heart
description: A heart-shaped rock hollow amid lush scenery in Japan.

name: Twenty-Six Martyrs Monument
description: A monument commemorating the execution of 26 canonized Christians from the 16th century.

name: Mount Asahi
description: The highest mountain in Hokkaido was the setting for one of Japan’s strangest unsolved mysteries.

name: The Dutch Slope
description: A historical western settlement that is known as one of the three most disappointing sights of Japan.

name: Dr. William S. Clark Statue
description: A statue of the forgotten American who helped transform Hokkaido and Japan.

name: Funazuka Burial Mound
description: Once a heap of dirt with a shrubbery on it, this unassuming mound in a parking lot turned out to be an ancient tumulus.

name: Denderano
description: According to local legend, elderly villagers were once brought to this hill and left to die.

name: Morse Seaside Laboratory Site
description: The oft-overlooked site of one of the first marine biological research facilities in the world.

name: Bronze Finials on the Upper Bridge
description: A rare surviving set of early 17th-century bronze finials remain on this bridge.

name: Kafū Nagai’s Study
description: In an unassuming corner of the city hall sits the restored study of a local author.

name: Ken Shimura Statue
description: This statue of what appears to be a feudal lord is actually a tribute to Japan's most beloved comedian.

name: Siebold Memorial Museum
description: A memorial to the man who brought Western medicine to Japan.

name: Ni-no-maru garden
description: A collection of trees from each of the 47 Japanese prefectures.

name: Glover Garden
description: The oldest western-style houses in Japan are centered around the industrialist Thomas Glover.

name: Kabukicho Robot Restaurant
description: Cabaret where the show girls are all androids, and the patrons couldn't be more pleased.

name: Anata No Warehouse
description: This faux-seedy Japanese arcade is made up to look like a maze of alleys straight out of a cyberpunk dystopia.

name: Fantasy Dining Alice in a Labyrinth
description: Fine dining in the bizarre world of Lewis Carroll.

name: Kawaii Monster Cafe
description: A Japanese concept restaurant that feels like a party thrown by the monsters in your closet.

name: Nakagin Capsule Tower
description: The original "capsule building" is a masterpiece of Metabolist architecture.

name: Zauo
description: The freshest fish here are the ones you catch yourself.

name: Dog's Roasted Sweet Potato Stand
description: The only thing sweeter than the potatoes is the canine proprietor of this roadside stall.

name: Nara Dreamland
description: The abandoned Japanese amusement park once loomed as a dystopian Disneyland.

name: Clone Factory
description: Japanese company creates clone dolls that reside in the "uncanny valley."

name: Kagaya
description: Serving you alcohol with a side of insanity. Also, costumes!

name: Asahi Plaza Capsule Hotel
description: One of the first hotel rooms in a pod.

name: Christon Café
description: A Catholicism-themed restaurant where sacrilege is on the menu.

name: Brasserie Kyushoku Toban
description: A tiny cafe that recreates the Japanese elementary school lunch experience.

name: Alcatraz ER
description: Japan's corrupt-mental-institution themed restaurant offers diners a chance to eat inside of filthy cells.

name: Ukiyoe Small Museum
description: A tiny collection of traditional Japanese woodblock prints, open only when the owner feels like it.

name: Mistral Bleu Train Bar
description: A cool dive bar, off the beaten track.

name: Noumin Café
description: This “peasant café” in the middle of Tokyo re-creates the atmosphere of the Japanese countryside.

name: Pasona O2
description: Underground farm located in a former bank vault deep within the bowels of Tokyo's business district.

name: Akeno Gekijo: Ruined Japanese Stripclub
description: This ruined Japanese stripclub sits half burnt to the ground.

name: Gajumaru Treehouse Diner
description: Okinawan dining in a twenty foot tall, man-made tree house overlooking Naha Harbor.

name: The Queen Chateau Soapland
description: The ruins of Japan’s premier water brothel.

name: Yurei Izakaya
description: Haunted house kitsch meets Japanese folklore at this theme bar where patrons can “die” and have their own mock funeral.

name: "Ju-On: The Grudge" House
description: An infamous house made famous by director Takashi Shimizu.

name: The Pearl Love Hotel
description: The ruin of a Japanese love hotel almost completely overgrown with brambles.

name: The Akasaka Love Hotel
description: The demolished ruins of a poorly located Japanese Love Hotel.

name: Tateishi Burger Vending Machine
description: A charming, homemade vending machine dispenses burgers and sandwiches at this hole-in-the-wall bakery.

name: Osaka Maritime Museum
description: An abandoned giant glass dome sits in the bay in “the Venice of the East.”

name: Niigata Russian Village
description: A failed Russian themed village and amusement park in Niigata prefecture.

name: Tokyo Tower Wax Museum's Progressive Rock Room
description: Unlikely place for krautrock legends to hang out.

name: Pink Holiday
description: Barbies stare from the walls, sit on the tables, and even pop out of cakes at this quirky cafe.

name: Tenkaen (China Park of Heaven)
description: After a short 5 year run, this former major theme park sits in ruin.

name: Tohoku New Zealand Village
description: A derelict New Zealand-themed amusement park in Japan.

name: Kewpie Itami Mayonnaise Factory
description: The Japanese mayo factory famous for its cute mascot.

name: 'Astro Boy Mosaic'
description: Japan's iconic sci-fi hero rendered to 8-bit by notorious French street artist Invader.

name: Clinton Home
description: An exact replica of Bill Clinton's childhood home stands within a resort in Japan.

name: 007 Museum
description: A museum dedicated to the least secret spy is actually an attempt to get Hollywood's attention.
